ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning Anger.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Anger is the ebullition of the heart’s blood produced by bilious exhalation or turbidity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Hence it is that the words χολή and χόλος are both used in the sense of anger.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Anger is sometimes lust for vengeance.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For when we are wronged or think that we are wronged, we are distressed, and there arises this mixture of desire and anger.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: There are three forms of anger: rage, which the Greeks also call χολή or χόλος, μῆνις and κότος.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: When anger arises and begins to be roused, it is called rage or χολή or χόλος.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Wrath again implies that the bile endures, that is to say, that the memory of the wrong abides: and indeed the Greek word for it, μῆνις is derived from μένειν, and means what abides and is transferred to memory.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Rancour, on the other hand, implies watching for a suitable moment for revenge, and the Greek word for it is κότος from κεῖσθαι.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Anger further is the satellite of reason, the vindicator of desire.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For when we long after anything and are opposed in our desire by some one, we are angered at that person, as though we had been wronged: and reason evidently deems that there are just grounds for displeasure in what has happened, in the case of those who, like us, have in the natural course of things to guard their own position.
